config.title: Is Berlin Walkable? FAQ
config.subtitle: A local guide's plain answers on walking around Berlin in 2026: how far apart the main sights really are, what you can see on foot in a day, and when to let the U-Bahn take the strain.

items.a: Yes, the central Mitte district is very walkable. Berlin is flat, the streets are wide and straight, and most of the famous sights, from the Reichstag and Brandenburg Gate to Museum Island and Alexanderplatz, sit within about a 25-minute walk of each other. The catch is that Berlin is a large city, so while you walk comfortably inside a district, you are better off taking the U-Bahn or S-Bahn to jump between districts.

items.q: How long does it take to walk from the Brandenburg Gate to Alexanderplatz?
items.a: About 2.5 km, or roughly 30 to 35 minutes of walking, straight down Unter den Linden and past Museum Island and the Berliner Dom. It is one of the best walks in the city because you pass the university, the opera, Bebelplatz and the cathedral on the way. It is very doable one way; many visitors walk out and take the tram or U-Bahn back.

items.q: Can you see Berlin's main sights on foot in one day?
items.a: Yes, the central spine works well on foot: the Reichstag, the Brandenburg Gate, the Holocaust Memorial, Unter den Linden, Museum Island and Alexanderplatz are all connected by an easy, flat walk. Allow at least three to four hours, longer with stops. Sights farther out, like the East Side Gallery, Charlottenburg Palace or a Sachsenhausen day trip, need the S-Bahn or U-Bahn rather than your feet.

items.q: Which Berlin sights are too far to walk between?
items.a: Anything past about 35 to 40 minutes on foot. The Brandenburg Gate to the East Side Gallery is around 4.8 km, close to an hour's walk, so take the U-Bahn or S-Bahn towards Warschauer Straße instead. Charlottenburg Palace, the Olympiastadion and out-of-town day trips are all firmly transit distances. A single AB ticket covers these hops.

items.q: Is central Berlin flat and easy to walk?
items.a: Yes, Berlin is famously flat, so there are no hills to climb and the walking is gentle. The main things to watch are the cobblestones around Museum Island and the Nikolaiviertel, which are hard on thin soles, and the red cycle lanes that often run along the pavement. Comfortable, sturdy shoes make a bigger difference than anything else.

items.q: Do I need public transport if I plan to walk around Berlin?
items.a: For the central sights, often not for a half day. But a single AB ticket or a day ticket earns its place for the longer hops, like the airport, the East Side Gallery, Charlottenburg or the summer lakes, and for tired legs at the end of a long day. The smart plan is to walk the connected core and ride the gaps.
